_doc: Editor steering notes for the conference preview agent. Authoritative — these override prior knowledge and any conflicting context-data interpretation. Keep each note to 1–3 short sentences. Empty strings are dropped silently. Loaded by build_conference_context.py from /cfb-research/writer_notes/<season>/<conf>.json.
_usage: conference_note: 1–3 sentences with two purposes — (a) the meta-narrative for the year (e.g., 'The realignment dust has settled but the talent gap between the top three and the rest is still widening.'), and (b) calibration the agent needs to right-size confidence: what 'good' looks like in this league. SEC example: 'In modern SEC: 7-1 in conf typically reaches the title game; 9-0 has never happened. 9 overall wins is a historic season.' MAC example: 'In the MAC, 8 conference wins is championship caliber; 6-2 contends.' Combine narrative + calibration when both matter.

team_notes.<slug>: 1–3 sentences about that specific team — what the data won't tell the agent (a coaching change, a critical transfer, an injury, an off-field story, the angle the writeup should take).
Slugs match the canonical team slugs in build_team_context.py / scrape_team_context.py.
conference_note: The ACC is a P4 conference but trails the Big Ten and SEC in talent and funding, with the bottom of the league usually housing the worst P4 teams in the country. Three storylines define the 2026 ACC: (1) [TBD — pick a 2026-specific narrative: Belichick year 2 at UNC, FSU's direction, Clemson in the NIL era, or similar]; (2) [TBD — second 2026 narrative]; (3) Miami's title-game runner-up encore — they made the national championship game in 2025 and lost to Indiana; the question is whether they can repeat the run or if last year was a peak that's hard to maintain. Conference-record calibration: the ACC title game is determined by 8-game conference record, not overall record. Duke proved the structural quirk in 2025 — a 6-2 conference record won the league despite a 7-5 overall regular season (one non-conference win all year). When framing records here, lead with the conference number, not the overall — a 7-2 conference team is a title-game contender even if they're 8-4 overall. Volatility calibration: the ACC is the most parity-driven of the P4 leagues. Outside of a small group of stable programs at the top, the rest of the league churns through tiers year-to-year, with QB health and two coin-flip games typically separating an 8-win season from a 4-win season. When team_notes give a 3-tier range (e.g., Tier 2-4), that's not a hedge — it's the league norm. The agent should NOT over-anchor on a single 'centering' tier; instead treat the range itself as the projection. A team at Tier 2-4 means 'either it works and they're contending for the title game or it doesn't and they're fighting for a bowl' — both branches are real. ACC tier framework (tiers describe team caliber, NOT exact record): Tier 1 = ACC title-game favorite if healthy and CFP-bound (typically as the conference auto-bid, not an at-large); Tier 2 = title-game contender, dark-horse CFP at-large; Tier 3 = middle tier and title-game outsider, in the contention mix but outside CFP; Tier 4 = rebuilding year, fighting to get to a bowl game; Tier 5 = down year, where reaching bowl eligibility would itself be the ceiling. When team_notes give a range, the agent should treat the wider boundary as the realistic downside — but per the volatility note above, 3-tier ranges are common here and the range itself should be framed as the projection rather than collapsing to a single tier.
